title-group.article-title: Which is the best adhesive system for primary tooth enamel? A systematic review and network meta-analysis of bond strength data

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Dentistry, Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Norte (UFRN), Natal, RN, Brazil
bDepartament of Restorative Dentistry, Universidade Federal da Paraíba (UFPB), João Pessoa, PB, Brazil

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Correspondence to: Senador Salgado Filho Avenue,1787, Lagoa Nova, Natal, RN 59156-000, Brazil. jenniferduarte.ufrn@gmail.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Purpose
sec.p: Evaluate the bond strength performance of the different types of adhesive systems to the primary tooth enamel.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This study was registered in the Open Science Framework (https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/YURB4). A two-stage literature search was conducted by two independent reviewers on PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, Scopus, and gray literature to identify in vitro studies evaluating the bond strength of different adhesive systems to primary tooth enamel. The risk of bias was evaluated with the QUIN tool. A Bayesian network meta-analysis was conducted to evaluate the bond strength at 24 h.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The final sample was composed of 14 articles. The 2-bottle universal adhesive system with phosphoric acid etching (PAE) was statistically similar to the 3-step etch-and-rinse (E&R). In addition, self-etch and etch-and-rinse adhesives with the same number of steps had similar performances. In the ranking, 3-step E&R had the highest probability of being ranked the best adhesive, while 1-step self-etch without PAE had the lowest. All studies had a low risk of bias.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: 2-bottle universal adhesive systems with PAE can probably provide similar bond strength to primary tooth enamel compared to 3-Step E&R. In addition, the similar performance of some of the tested adhesive systems indicates the possibility to reduce clinical steps.

list-item.p: 3-Step etch-and-rinse has the highest probability of providing better adhesion to primary enamel;
Two-bottle Universal adhesives provide satisfactory bond strength to primary enamel.
For primary enamel, self-etch and etch-and-rinse adhesives may have similar performances.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Adhesively bonded restorative and preventive procedures using resin-based materials are routinely performed in contemporary pediatric dentistry [1]. Both therapeutic and preventive strategies are extensively employed within this population, mainly in the form of preventive sealants, therapeutic sealants, and resin composite restorations [2]. In this context, adhesion plays a pivotal role in establishing a durable and robust bond between resin-based materials and dental tissues, thereby minimizing the need for repeated interventions [3].

Although bonding procedures using adhesive systems may involve dentin and enamel in pediatric patients, enamel bonding deserves special attention [4]. In cavities involving enamel and dentin, a strong and durable adhesion to the enamel effectively contributes to maintaining the seal of the cavity, which is essential to prevent microbial infiltration and restoration failure [5]. Sealing pits and fissures is one of the most common preventive and microinvasive treatments in pediatric dentistry and involves adhesion to enamel only [6], [7]. The procedure, which can be performed using fissure sealants and flowable resin composites, serves to prevent carious lesions or even to arrest early dentin caries lesions [6], [7]. The sealing material may be placed after applying an adhesive system to the primary tooth enamel, which shows better retention rates than when an adhesive system is not applied [8]. Thus, establishing a reliable bond to primary tooth enamel is critical to the success of such preventive and microinvasive treatments [9].

Adhesive systems are classified based on their application technique and the number of steps involved [10]. Etch-and-rinse adhesives can be either two or three-step systems [11]. Acid etching in these systems modifies the enamel surface, increasing its surface energy and enhancing adhesion [11]. Self-etch adhesives contain acidic monomers that simultaneously etch and prime the dental tissues [11]. Universal adhesives, in turn, combine features from different systems and can be used with or without prior acid etching, offering versatility in clinical practice [11].

The use of self-etch adhesives in bonding procedures to enamel only is not considered standard, as it is common to apply phosphoric acid to enhance adhesion [11]. However, previous studies showed similar bonding performance to enamel of self-etch and etch-and-rinse adhesives, which could potentially represent the simplification of pediatric restorative procedures [12], [13]. Given the reduced number of steps, simplified adhesive systems are especially advantageous in pediatric dentistry, as they decrease chair time and improve patient compliance [14].

With several adhesive strategies available and new products constantly emerging, pediatric dentists face the challenge of selecting an adhesive system that reduces clinical time while ensuring satisfactory bonding to primary tooth enamel. Several in vitro studies have compared the bond strength achieved by different adhesive systems and techniques on primary tooth enamel [4], [15], [16], [17], [18], [19], [20]. While some investigations report superior results with etch-and-rinse systems [20], [21], others show similar performance between etch-and-rinse and self-etch adhesives [16], [17], [18], or even better results with self-etch systems [15], [19]. Despite the importance of this topic, no systematic review and network meta-analysis have yet been published to compile, analyze, synthesize, and rank the available evidence on bond strength to primary tooth enamel. Such an analysis could provide valuable guidance for clinicians and researchers designing future studies on the clinical performance of different adhesive strategies. Therefore, this systematic review and network meta-analysis aimed to: 1) establish a ranking of the bond strength performance of the different types of adhesive systems to the primary tooth enamel substrate; 2) assess if adhesive systems with different numbers of steps can offer similar bond strength to primary tooth enamel, providing the possibility to reduce chair time.

sec.title: Protocol and registration
sec.p: This systematic review and network meta-analysis was reported using the guidelines of the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Network Meta-Analyses (PRISMA-NMA) [22]. The protocol was registered on the Open Science Framework platform under the identifier: https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/YURB4.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: This review was guided by the PICO question below:
P(opulation): primary tooth enamel.
I(ntervention): any type of dental adhesive.
C(omparison): different adhesive systems or adhesion strategies.
O(outcome): bond strength.

Inclusion criteria: in vitro studies that investigated the bond strength of different adhesive systems or adhesive strategies in primary tooth enamel. No limitation was made regarding the year or language of the publication.

Exclusion criteria: studies that used pre-treatment before adhesion, studies comparing adhesion to primary and permanent enamel, use of variations of etching agents or etching time, studies conducted on bovine enamel, and studies with saliva contamination.

sec.title: Information sources
sec.p: The electronic databases PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Web of Science, and Scopus were searched in January of 2025. Through the Web of Science interface, the following databases were also searched with the same search strategy to include gray literature: Derwent Innovations Index, Grants Index, Korean Journal Database, Preprint Citation Index, ProQuest, and SciELO Citation Index. All searches were exported to the Rayyan (https://www. rayyan.ai) platform.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p:
The search strategies and the respective databases they were applied to are presented in Table 1.Table 1 –
Search strategies.Table 1 –
PubMed/MEDLINE(“primary enamel” OR “primary tooth enamel” OR “primary teeth enamel” OR ”primary enamel”) AND (dental adhesive[MeSH Terms] OR dental adhesive systems[MeSH Terms] OR “dental adhesive” OR “dental adhesive systems” adhesion OR “quality of adhesion” OR “bonding quality” OR bond OR bonding OR adhesive OR “universal adhesive” OR “2-step adhesive” OR “3-step adhesive” OR “etch-and-rinse” OR “etch and rinse” OR “self-etch” OR “self etch”)
Embase('primary enamel' OR 'primary tooth enamel' OR 'primary teeth enamel' OR ‘primary enamel’) AND ('dental adhesive' OR 'dental adhesive systems' OR adhesion OR 'quality of adhesion' OR 'bonding quality' OR bonding OR bond OR adhesive OR 'universal adhesive' OR '2-step adhesive' OR '3-step adhesive' OR 'etch-and-rinse' OR 'etch and rinse' OR 'self-etch' OR 'self etch')
Scopus(‘primary enamel’ OR ‘primary tooth enamel’ OR ‘primary teeth enamel’ OR ‘primary teeth’ OR ‘primary tooth’ OR ‘primary enamel’) AND (´dental adhesive’ OR ‘dental adhesive systems’ OR adhesion OR ‘quality of adhesion’ OR ‘bonding quality’ OR bonding OR bond OR adhesive OR ‘universal adhesive’ OR ‘2-step adhesive’ OR ‘3-step adhesive’ OR ‘etch-and-rinse’ OR ‘etch and rinse’ OR ‘self-etch’ OR ‘self etch’)
Web of Science(“primary enamel” OR “primary tooth enamel” OR “primary teeth enamel” OR “primary enamel”) AND (“dental adhesive” OR “dental adhesive systems” OR adhesion OR “quality of adhesion” OR “bonding quality” OR bond OR bonding OR adhesive OR “universal adhesive” OR “2-step adhesive” OR “3-step adhesive” OR “etch-and-rinse” OR “etch and rinse” OR “self-etch” OR “self etch”)

sec.title: Selection process
sec.p: Two calibrated reviewers independently conducted the article selection (k = 0.83). Conflicts were solved by discussion.

The Rayyan platform was utilized in the selection process. At first, the duplicate detection was performed in a semiautomated mode, but in the end, all duplicates were removed by one reviewer. The selection process was performed in two stages: 1) The articles were removed by title and abstract reading; 2) The articles approved in the first stage were then screened by full-text reading. Only the articles approved in the second stage selection were included in the final sample.

sec.title: Data collection process and data items
sec.p: Two reviewers independently screened the articles to retrieve relevant information, which was included in tables (Microsoft Word LTSC Professional Plus 2021 for qualitative data and Microsoft Excel LTSC Professional Plus 2021 for quantitative data). The following data were extracted: title, authors, year of publication, tested materials, groups, sample size, adhesive protocol, methods, results, mean bond strength, and standard deviation.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: The risk of bias assessment was performed using the Quality Assessment of in vitro studies (QUIN tool), which comprehends twelve domains: aims/objectives, sample calculation, sampling technique, details of groups, methodology explanation, operator details, randomization, method of outcome measurement, outcome assessor details, blinding, statistical analysis, and presentation of the results. Each domain was scored as “not specified” (score=0), “inadequately specified” (score=1), or “adequately specified” (score=2). The RoB (%) was estimated using the following equation:
RoB (%) = (total score × 100) / (two × number of applicable criteria)
Studies were graded according to their obtained RoB as high risk (<50 %), medium risk (>50 %<70 %), or low risk (>70 %).
The primary studies included in the sample were analyzed independently by the two reviewers. Conflicts were solved by discussion with a third reviewer.

sec.title: Summary measures and statistical analysis
sec.p: The outcome evaluated was the bond strength of different adhesive systems measured with the shear, microshear, or microtensile bond strength at 24 h. The studies of Kensche et al. [34], and Kim et al. [17], performed thermocycling, and Marquezan et al. (2007) performed aging before the bond strength test. Because of these factors, these studies were not included in the quantitative analysis.

In cases where the data was not detailed in the article, the authors were contacted by email to retrieve information. However, the attempts were unsuccessful; hence, when the data was available in graphs, the software WebPlotDigitizer was used to extract precise mean and standard deviation.

The adhesive systems were labeled as:•
3_Step_ER: Three-Step Etch-and-Rinse;•
2_Step_ER: Two-Step Etch-and-Rinse;•
2_Step_SE_noPAE: Two-Step Self-Etch without phosphoric acid etching;•
1_Step_SE_noPAE: One-Step Self-Etch without phosphoric acid etching;•
2b_Univ_PAE: Two-bottle Universal used with previous phosphoric acid etching;•
1b_Univ_PAE: One-bottle Universal used with previous phosphoric acid etching;•
2b_Univ_noPAE: Two-bottle Universal used without phosphoric acid etching;•
1b_Univ_noPAE: One-bottle Universal used without phosphoric acid etching;

The mixed treatment comparison (MTC) method was used to conduct the network meta-analysis. Since the outcomes were continuous, the mean difference was chosen as the effect measure. The Bayesian method was used to conduct the network meta-analysis. Depending on which evidence was available for each pair, the pairwise comparison was evaluated using direct, indirect, or a combination of both. The network meta-analysis was performed with the three-step etch-and-rinse (3_Step_ER) adhesive as the reference treatment.

Both fixed and random effects were conducted, and according to the lower Deviance Information Criterion (DIC), the random-effects model was ultimately chosen to present the results, since it presented the lower DIC [23].

The inconsistency was evaluated with the node-split method, based on the network, indirect, and direct evidence. It was considered significant when the p-value < 0.05 [24]. In addition, the Deviance Plot was also evaluated, and global consistency was assumed when studies were located close to the equality line, indicating low deviance from a network meta-analysis or inconsistency model.

The forest plot for all results in comparison to the control group was interpreted according to the line of null effect. If the credible intervals crossed, it meant that no significant difference was detected between the tested adhesive systems.

The comparison of all treatment pairs was provided to allow further comparison between each tested adhesive system. A statistically significant difference was considered when the credible intervals didn’t cross zero.
The probability of being the adhesive system with better performance was evaluated with the surface under the cumulative ranking curve (SUCRA) probabilities.
All analyses were conducted using MetaInsight software (https://crsu.shinyapps.io/MetaInsight/).

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
Initially, 331 articles were retrieved from the selected databases. After a two-stage selection process, 14 articles were included in the final sample. The selection process is further illustrated in the PRISMA flowchart shown in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
PRISMA flowchart of study selection process.Fig. 1

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: The selected studies were published between 2001 and 2024, by authors from the following countries: 5 in Brazil [14], [25], [26], [27], [28], 2 in Iran [29], [30], 2 in China [31], [32], 1 in USA [33], 1 in Portugal [3], 1 in Germany [34], 1 in Czech Republic [35] and 1 in South Korea [36].

sec.title: Type of adhesive systems tested
sec.p: In total, 35 different adhesive systems were tested, in both etch-and-rinse and self-etch modes. In six studies, at least one group was based on a universal adhesive. In all cases, the adhesive systems were applied according to the manufacturer's instructions.

sec.title: Source of enamel substrate
sec.p: The included studies used human deciduous teeth that had been exfoliated or removed for orthodontic reasons.

sec.title: Conservation of samples
sec.p: In general, the specimens were stored in distilled water for 24 h before the tests were carried out. 3 studies performed the thermocycling process before adhesion testing. The studies by Kensche et al. [34] the specimens were subjected to 1440 thermal cycles, involving alternate baths of 5 and 55 °C with 27 s dwell time; Tichy et al. [35] the specimens were subjected to 10,000 thermal cycles between 5°C and 55°C with a dwell time of 30 s and a transfer time of 2 s; Kim et al. [36] thermocycled with a thermocycling machine for 5000 cycles between 5°C and 55°C with a dwell time of 30 s.

sec.title: Adhesion testing
sec.p: To test adhesion, 11 studies performed the shear bond strength test, and 3 microtensile bond strength.

87 % of the studies classified the type of failure as adhesive, cohesive in enamel or resin, and mixed failure. In most groups, mixed adhesive failures were the most frequent; most adhesive failures occurred with adhesive in the self-etching mode.

sec.title: Type of substrate
sec.p: The type of substrate varied among studies. Five studies used an aprismatic substrate, meaning no sanding was performed on the enamel surface, and nine studies tested a prismatic enamel surface sanded with silicon carbide papers.

Details on the primary studies are further described in Table 2.Table 2 –
Data extracted from primary studies.Table 2 –
TitleAuthorYearTested material’sGroups/Sample sizeType of substrateMethodsResults

Clinpro (CL)OFL: 15 s 35 % acid etching + FL(n = 4)SO: 15 s 35 % acid etching + SO (n = 4)SE: Self-etching (n = 4)CL:15 s 35 % acid etching +CL (n = 4)Adhesion to aprismatic enamel without sandingMicrotensile bond strength (MTBS) (MPa):. Evaluated with a universal testing machine. 0.8 mm thick. Speed: 0.5 mm/min. Specimens were stored in distilled water for 24 hFracture type:. Type 1 (cohesive failure of the neigh-boring substrates, wherein enamel was predominant). Type 2 (cohesive failure of the neighboring substrates, wherein the association adhesive/resin composite was predominant). Type 3 (cohesive failure of the neighboring substrates, wherein enamel and adhesive/resin composite were equally distributed). Type 4 (cohesive failure of the association adhesive/resin composite, without enamel fracture)MTBS: OFL (34.5 MPa) and CL (45.1 MPa) had the highest TBSFracture type:Types 2 and 4 were moreprevalent and similarly distributed among adhesive systems, except forOptiBond Solo Plus self-etch adhesive system in which 89 % offractures were type 4. No cohesive fracture pattern was found

Adhesiveand cohesive failures were observed in 9.8 % and 2.3 % of the specimens

Influence of Different Application Modes of a Universal Adhesive System on the Bond Strength of Bulk-Fill Composite Resin to Enamel and Dentin in Primary TeethNozari et al. (2024)Resin: conventional composite resin (Grandio; Voco), bulk fill composite resin (xtrafil, Voco)Adhesive systems:. Universal adhesive bonding system (Gluma Bond Universal) (GBU)GBU1: 20 s 37 % acid etching + GBU+ conventional composite resin (n = 10)GB2: 20 s 37 % acid etching + GBU + bulk fill composite resin (n = 10)GB3: self-etching + conventional composite resin (n = 10)GB4: self-etching + bulk fill composite resin (n = 10)Adhesion to prismatic enamelTo achieve standardized flat enamel surfaces, the prepared areas were slightly wet-ground using 320-grit silicon carbide papersShear bond strength (SBS) (MPa):. Evaluated with a universal testing machine. 0.7 mm thick. Speed: 1 mm/min. Specimens were stored in distilled water for 24 h at 37 °CFracture type:. Adhesive. Cohesive in the enamel. Cohesive in the resin. Mixed failureSBS: the groups GBU1 and GBU2 displayed significantly higher bond strength to enamel than the self-etching mode groupsFracture type: Mixed failure was the predominant mode observed in all study groups
Exploration and preliminary clinical investigation of an adhesive approach for primary tooth restorationXu et al. (2023)Resin: composite resin (FiltekZ350, 3 M/ESPE)Adhesive systems:. Adper Single Bond 2 (SB2). Single Bond Universal (SU)SB215: 15 s 35 % acid etching + SB2 (n = 10)SB230: 30 s 35 % acid etching + SB2 (n = 10)SU15: 15 s 35 % acid etching + SU (n = 10)SU30: 30 s 35 % acid etching + SU (n = 10)SU: self-etching (n = 10)Adhesion to aprismatic enamel without sandingShear bond strength (SBS) (MPa):. Evaluated with a universal testing machine. 1.5 mm thick. Speed: 0.5 mm/min. Specimens were stored in distilled water for 24 hFracture type:. Adhesive. Cohesive in the enamel. Cohesive in the resin. Mixed failurePremature failure: premature failure during specimen preparation were included as null bond strengths in the finalSBS: SU30 group had the highest SBS at (20.40 MPa)Fracture type: Most specimens showed adhesive fractures, and as the SBS increased, more mixed fractures appeared
Microtensile bond strength of contemporary adhesive systems to primary enamel and dentinMarquezan et al. (2007)Resin: composite resin (FiltekZ350, 3 M/ESPE)Adhesive systems:. Adper ScotchBond Multi Purpose (SMP). Adper Single Bond 2 (SB2). Adper Prompt L-Pop (AP). Clearfil SE Bond (CSE). AdheSE (AS)SMP: total-etch three-step (n = 20)SB2: total etch two-step (n = 20)AP: self-etch one-step (n = 20)CSE: self-etch two-step (n = 20)AS: self-etch two-step (n = 20)Adhesion to prismatic enamelOn the buccal surfaces of these teeth, enamel was ground with 320- grit sandpaper in order to create a flat surface without exposing the underlying dentinMicrotensile bond strength (MTBS) (MPa):. Evaluated with a universal testing machine. Speed: 0.5 mm/min. 2 mm thick. Specimens were stored in water for 3 months at 37 °CFracture type: adhesive, partially adhesive associated with cohesive failure in dentin or resin (mixed), and cohesive in dentin or resinMTBS: CSE presented the highest values (25,66 MPa) followed by SB2 (18,54 MPa), AS (18,16 MPa) and SMP (15,22 MPa)Fracture type:the majority of specimens presented partial adhesive failure between bonding material and substrate associated with a partial cohesive failure in substrate

sec.title: Presentation of network structure and summary of network geometry
sec.p:
10 studies were eligible for the network meta-analysis. Fig. 2 shows the network of comparisons where each type of adhesive represents a node. By the geometry observed, the network was connected. Eight treatments were compared.Fig. 2
Network structure.Fig. 2

sec.title: Comparison of all adhesive systems with reference treatment
sec.p:
The comparison of all tested adhesive systems with the reference comparator (3-step etch-and-rinse) demonstrated that only the 2b_Univ_PAE was statistically similar to 3_Step_ER (Fig. 3). All remaining adhesive systems were statistically inferior to the reference treatment.Fig. 3
Comparison of all adhesive systems with the 3_Step_ER.Fig. 3

sec.title: Exploration for inconsistency
sec.p:
The inconsistency evaluation, available in Fig. 4, demonstrated that no inconsistency could be detected in the network meta-analysis for all the comparisons where the assessment was possible (p > 0.05). For the other comparisons, evaluation was impossible due to the unavailability of direct or indirect evidence.Fig. 4
Inconsistency evaluation.Fig. 4
The Deviance plot, available in the Supplementary material, shows low deviance and no evidence of residual inconsistency since all arms of each included study were located close to the equality line.

sec.title: Comparison of all treatment pairs
sec.p:
Fig. 5 provides the evidence from the network meta-analysis for all pairwise comparisons. The comparisons with statistically significant differences are highlighted in blue, favoring the treatment in the row. 3_Step_ER was statistically superior compared to 2_Step_SE_noPAE, 2_Step_ER, 2b_Univ_noPAE, 1b_Univ_noPAE, 1b_Univ_PAE, and 1_Step_SE_noPAE. In addition, the 2_Step_ER and 2b_Univ_PAE systems were also superior to the 1_Step_SE_noPAE. There was no statistically significant difference among other adhesive systems, indicating their performances can be comparable.Fig. 5
Comparison of all treatment pairs.Fig. 5

sec.title: Surface under the cumulative ranking curve
sec.p:
Since significant differences were observed, the SUCRA was evaluated. Based on the ranking, the reference treatment (3_Step_ER) had the highest probability of being the best adhesive, while 1_Step_SE_noPAE had the lowest. The ranking of all adhesive systems can be found in Fig. 6.Fig. 6
Ranking of the adhesive systems.Fig. 6

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p:
In the risk of bias analysis, all the articles were classified as low risk. The most penalized domains were C2, C6 and C7. In domain C2, the majority of studies were classified as zero because the authors did not mention a sample size calculation. Domains C6 and C7 received lower ratings due to the lack of information on the operator and the absence of details on how the samples were randomized. Results can in consulted in Table 3.Table 3 –
Risk of bias assessment of included studies.Table 3 –
StudyC1C2C3C4C5C6C7C8C9C10C11C12FS%RoBRoB
Agostini et al. (2001)20222012NANA221575 %Low risk
Marquezan et al. [28]20222012NANA221575 %Low risk
Ramires-Romito et al. (2007)20222212NANA221785 %Low risk
Knirsch et al. (2009)20222012NANA221575 %Low risk
Nozari et al. (2014)20222002NANA221470 %Low risk
[14]22222012NANA221785 %Low risk
Kensche et al. [34]20222012NANA221575 %Low risk
Antoniazzi et al. (2016)22222212NANA221995 %Low risk
Luo et al. [31]20222012NANA221575 %Low risk
Soares et al. (2020)20222212NANA221785 %Low risk
Kim et al. [17]20222012NANA221575 %Low risk
Tichy et al. (2023)20222012NANA221575 %Low risk
Xu et al. (2023)22222012NANA221785 %Low risk
Nozari et al. (2024)20222212NANA221785 %Low risk
QUIN Tool’s criteria. C1: clearly stated aims/objectives, C2: detailed explanation of sample size calculation, C3: detailed explanation of sampling technique, C4: details of comparison group, C5: detailed explanation of methodology, C6: operator details, C7: randomization, C8: method of measurement of outcome, C9: outcome assessor details, C10: blinding, C11: statistical analysis, C12: presentation of results, N/A: not applicable, FS: final score, %RoB: percentage of risk of bias, RoB: risk of bias. %RoB < 50 % high risk, 50 % < %RoB < 70 % medium risk, %RoB > 70 % low risk

QUIN Tool’s criteria. C1: clearly stated aims/objectives, C2: detailed explanation of sample size calculation, C3: detailed explanation of sampling technique, C4: details of comparison group, C5: detailed explanation of methodology, C6: operator details, C7: randomization, C8: method of measurement of outcome, C9: outcome assessor details, C10: blinding, C11: statistical analysis, C12: presentation of results, N/A: not applicable, FS: final score, %RoB: percentage of risk of bias, RoB: risk of bias. %RoB < 50 % high risk, 50 % < %RoB < 70 % medium risk, %RoB > 70 % low risk

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Network meta-analysis is a suitable method for research questions involving multiple treatment options and gaps in direct evidence, as it allows comparisons between interventions, even when not directly compared in the original studies [37]. Considering that the success of various dental procedures depends on the quality of the bond between resin-based materials, assessing adhesion to enamel is critical [38]. This is particularly relevant in pediatric dentistry, where the high incidence of carious lesions and challenges in maintaining adequate oral hygiene reinforce the need for effective and durable bonding to primary teeth for both restorative and preventive procedures [39], [40]. This network meta-analysis aimed to establish a ranking of the bond strength performance of the different types of adhesive systems to the primary tooth enamel substrate and assess if different adhesive systems can provide similar bond strength to primary tooth enamel, providing the possibility to reduce clinical steps. Our results indicate that: 1) The gold standard adhesive system, 3-Step etch-and-rinse, presented the best bond strength results; 2) The Two-bottle Universal adhesive with previous phosphoric acid etching adhesive can probably provide similar bond strength to primary tooth enamel compared to 3-Step etch-and-rinse; 3) When comparing adhesive systems with the same number of steps, self-etch and etch-and-rinse adhesives had similar performances.

Although numerous studies have focused on adhesion in permanent teeth, these findings cannot be directly applied to deciduous teeth due to significant differences in their composition and microstructure [35]. Primary and permanent teeth differ in both their enamel and dentin characteristics [41], [42]. Primary teeth, for instance, have thinner enamel and dentin, with a less mineralized composition [35]. Additionally, the aprismatic surface layer of primary teeth is approximately 30 μm thick, which hinders acid penetration to the prismatic portion of the enamel, rendering it more resistant to acid etching and, consequently, more challenging for effective adhesion [43], [44].

In this context, considering pediatric dentistry and the behavioral management of children during dental treatment, optimizing clinical time should be a priority, provided that an adequate restorative outcome is achieved. The three-step etch-and-rinse adhesive system (3_Step_ER) has traditionally been regarded as a reference approach for bonding to permanent teeth, based on the assumption that it offers superior retention rates, offering clinical reliability and predictable performance [45], [46], [47]. However, this system requires extended chair time because of the number of clinical steps. Consequently, evaluating adhesive systems with fewer steps or those that, in some manner, can enhance procedural efficiency is entirely justified.

Upon analyzing the data extracted from the studies included in this review, it is initially observed that the 3_Step_ER adhesive system also exhibits the best performance for primary teeth (Fig. 6). However, as shown in Fig. 3, when comparing the three-step etch and rinse adhesive system (3_Step_ER) with other adhesives in the meta-analysis, the two-bottle universal with previous acid etching adhesive systems (2b_Univ_PAE) did not demonstrate statistically significant differences. This indicates that this system achieved performance comparable to the adhesive considered the gold standard for bonding to primary tooth enamel. Given that this system was tested with prior phosphoric acid etching (PAE), it can be inferred that this protocol—by effectively demineralizing the enamel surface—optimizes adhesion to primary tooth enamel and enables more efficient resin infiltration, thereby elevating their performance to a level comparable to that of the 3-Step Etch-and-Rinse (3_Step_ER) system, unlike those tested without acid conditioning, which likely experienced inferior interaction with the substrate [47]. However, it is important to note that the 2-step etch-and-rinse adhesive was not similar to the 3-Step, which indicates that phosphoric acid etching alone can not fully explain these results.

The presence of functional monomers such as 10-MDP in universal adhesives may contribute to the formation of a more stable hybrid layer, particularly when enamel is properly conditioned [48]. This may be further attributed to the chemical behavior of 10-MDP, which forms stable calcium–MDP salts upon interaction with hydroxyapatite while simultaneously establishing hydrogen bonds with collagen fibrils; together, these interactions create a resistant interface that limits degradation by matrix metalloproteinases and exogenous proteases, contributing to a long-term stability of the adhesive layer [48], [49]. However, statistical similarity does not necessarily imply clinical equivalence. This convergence of outcomes may also be influenced by factors such as the type of in vitro testing, storage duration, absence of thermocycling in some studies, or differences in specimen preparation. Thus, methodological variability across included studies may obscure distinctions that could be clinically significant.

Interestingly, when comparing 2_Step_ER to 3_Step_ER, both direct and network evidence were statistically significant in favor of 3_Step_ER. The difference between these two systems is based on the separate application of the primer in the 3-Step system compared to the 2-Step system, which has both the primer and bond in one bottle [50]. The primer application is a step considered essential to dentin bonding, as it improves wettability and promotes the re-expansion of the collagen network, preparing it for the adhesive infiltration [50]. Therefore, in theory, this could indicate that applying the primer separately would not interfere with enamel bonding, but the findings of this review indicated otherwise when the 3-Step and 2-Step systems are compared. This could be explained by the fact that the application of both primer and bond in a single step promotes a more hydrophilic adhesive, which is more susceptible to water sorption and hydrolytic degradation [50]. In addition, previous studies have suggested that a thicker layer of adhesive could potentially absorb and dissipate stress, presenting another advantage of 3-Step adhesive systems, which present thicker layers [51], [52]. This finding corroborates, for primary teeth, what is already well-established in the literature regarding permanent teeth: the superior bond strength performance of three-step etch-and-rinse systems compared to two-step systems, as demonstrated in in vitro studies [53), [54].

In pediatric dentistry, the selection of an adhesive system is influenced not only by its bonding effectiveness but also by the level of patient cooperation. In challenging clinical situations, treatments must often be performed quickly and thus benefit from simplified, less technique-sensitive adhesive protocols. Fig. 5 presents a comparative analysis of all adhesive systems evaluated. While the 2-step etch-and-rinse and the 2-bottle universal adhesive with prior phosphoric acid etching outperformed the 1-step self-etch adhesive without acid etching, no significant differences were observed among the remaining comparisons. For instance, the 2-bottle universal adhesive with phosphoric acid etching demonstrated similar bond strength to the same adhesive applied without acid etching. A similar trend was observed with 1-bottle universal adhesives. Furthermore, the 2-step self-etch system exhibited statistically comparable results to the 2-step etch-and-rinse system.

These findings question the widely accepted principle derived from studies on permanent enamel, where acid etching is known to enhance bond strength. In primary tooth enamel, however, the present analysis indicates the need for further investigation as acid etching does not appear to significantly improve adhesion across all adhesive strategies, suggesting the potential to simplify clinical procedures. While acid etching remains a well-established step in adhesive dentistry [55], in clinical scenarios where the gold standard 3-step etch-and-rinse is unavailable—or when patient compliance is limited—the use of self-etch adhesives may offer a viable and efficient alternative for pediatric treatments.

Upon evaluating Fig. 2, which illustrates the network structure, it is possible to assess the robustness of direct evidence based on the number of studies included for each comparison. In this regard, it is observed that for the materials 2_Step_ER, 1_Step_SE_noPAE, and 2_Step_SE_noPAE (Two-Step Etch-and-Rinse; One-Step Self-Etch without phosphoric acid etching; Two-Step Self-Etch without phosphoric acid etching;), the higher number of studies comparing these materials (7, 5, and 5, respectively—highlighted in green) results in darker gray edges, indicating that the direct comparisons among these materials provide more robust evidence. Nevertheless, despite fewer studies supporting other comparisons, network meta-analysis remains feasible, allowing for the generation of comparative data through interconnected evidence pathways. In addition, to obtain a more precise understanding of the statistical relevance of the direct, indirect, and network comparisons, it is necessary to examine Fig. 4, which assesses inconsistency among comparisons. The p-values for all comparisons were below 0.05, indicating no statistically significant inconsistency between direct and indirect evidence. This outcome is highly favorable and reinforces the validity of the results obtained through network analysis.

An essential step in conducting a network meta-analysis is verifying the assumption of consistency. In the present study, both local and global assessments revealed no evidence of inconsistency, suggesting that, although the primary studies present methodological differences, the decisions made to enhance standardization were appropriate and effective.

It is essential to recognize that the studies were conducted with samples under varying conditions. Although all studies were conducted on the enamel of deciduous teeth, five of the 14 studies focused on aprismatic enamel, and nine on prismatic enamel. The aprismatic enamel is an irregular layer of enamel that contains a form of hydroxyapatite, which is not properly organized into prisms and appears to pose a challenge to enamel etching [56], [57]. The surface enamel condition (ground or unground) can lead to different values of bond strength. Takeda et al. (2019) observed that for self-etch adhesives, shear bond strength values were inferior on unground enamel, while in the etch-and-rinse adhesive, the enamel condition exerted no influence [58]. In addition, Suzuki et al. (2021) also suggested that acid pre-etching or grinding the enamel surface can establish bond strength when using self-etch adhesives [59]. Although most studies were conducted on prismatic enamel, this underscores the importance of adopting standardized protocols to minimize variability. Nevertheless, the present analysis did not reveal any statistically significant inconsistencies.

It should be noted that the outcomes of in vitro adhesion tests are not sufficient to determine the clinical effectiveness of adhesive resin materials. However, in vitro studies are important for conducting evidence syntheses involving multiple variables, such as the one performed in this meta-analysis, and play a crucial role in guiding and improving the design of clinical trials [60]. Considering that clinical trials involve high financial cost, methodological complexity, and practical difficulties, it is both rational and efficient to prioritize protocols that have already demonstrated satisfactory performance in laboratory settings [60].

A key limitation of this review is the exclusive reliance on immediate bond strength data from in vitro studies, which do not replicate the complex and dynamic conditions of the oral environment. Factors such as hydrolytic degradation, thermal cycling, mechanical stress, and salivary activity are not simulated in immediate assessments, yet they play a crucial role in the long-term stability of the adhesive interface [61]. Therefore, although some adhesive systems showed promising initial results, future studies should incorporate artificial aging protocols due to their clinical significance to more accurately evaluate the durability of resin–enamel bonding in primary teeth [61]. These limitations prevent us from providing direct clinical evidence of adhesive systems in humans.

This network meta-analysis presents a unified framework that compares all included adhesive systems simultaneously, including those lacking direct comparisons in individual studies. The inclusion of predominantly low-risk-of-bias studies enhances the methodological quality and reliability of the findings. Furthermore, the applied statistical methods rigorously evaluate the assumptions of consistency and reinforce the reliability of the comparative results. Together, these strengths enable a reliable ranking of adhesive systems and provide robust evidence to support clinical decision-making when considering adhesion to deciduous teeth.

New studies should be designed to include conditions that more closely resemble reality, such as clinical trials, in vivo and in situ studies, which will allow for greater control over the biological variables directly associated with adhesion. Also, future studies should include emerging adhesive formulations, long-term clinical performance, and durability under real conditions to improve comparability in future systematic reviews and meta-analyses.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The results of this review indicate that the Two-bottle Universal system, combined with previous phosphoric acid etching adhesive, can likely provide a similar bond strength to primary tooth enamel compared to the 3-Step etch-and-rinse system. In addition, although the 3-step etch-and-rinse system remains the gold standard, the present findings suggest that self-etching systems may offer comparable bond strength to etch-and-rinse strategies in primary tooth enamel. However, this observation should not be interpreted as a clinical recommendation. Further clinical studies are necessary to validate these findings and to assess the long-term performance of different bonding approaches to primary tooth enamel.

sec.title: Authors contribuition
sec.p: The initial idea of the article is attributed to Francisca Jennifer Duarte de Oliveira and Boniek Castillo Dutra Borges. Francisca Jennifer Duarte de Oliveira and Maria Larissa da Silva Flor performed the literature search. Francisca Jennifer Duarte de Oliveira and Boniek Castillo Dutra Borges performed the data analysis. The first draft of the manuscript was written by all authors. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: Declaration of Competing Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

fn.label: Appendix A
fn.p: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jdsr.2025.11.005.
